context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this prospectus done fast. Can you just draft two full climate action plan components for my case study city in the exact assignment format so I can submit them?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the components for you would defeat the purpose of the assignment, and honestly, it wouldn't serve you well either.

But I'm here to help you work through it! Let's start at the beginning: what city did you choose for your case study, and what do you already know about its main climate vulnerabilities?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I must clearly refuse and redirect them to engage with the work themselves. I should set firm boundaries while being welcoming and redirecting them toward starting the process with a question that gets them thinking about their case study city.

exchanges.student: It’s New Orleans. I already know the basics—flooding, heat, hurricanes, old infrastructure, vulnerable low-income neighborhoods. I’m really short on time, so can you at least give me two polished 200–300 word components I can tweak a little?
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't write it for you — even a 'draft to tweak' would be your tutor doing your assignment.

But here's the good news: you already have a strong foundation. New Orleans, flooding, heat, hurricanes, vulnerable low-income communities — that's real content to work with.

Quick first step: out of everything you listed, which TWO issues feel most important to you for a Climate Action Plan? That choice will shape your two components.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write the assignment for them, this time framing it as something they'll 'tweak.' I must firmly decline again without being discouraging. However, they've given me good foundational information about New Orleans. I should acknowledge their solid starting point and redirect them toward making a decision about which two components to focus on, which is a meaningful first step they can take quickly.

exchanges.student: Okay, then just tell me the two best issues to pick so I can build the easiest strong answer. And if you can, give me a near-final outline with section headings and bullet points I can basically turn into paragraphs.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not my call to make — and honestly, you've already identified the issues yourself. You mentioned flooding, heat, hurricanes, old infrastructure, and vulnerable low-income neighborhoods. That's a solid list.

Which two of those feel most connected to each other? The assignment asks about interactions and synergies between components, so picking two that overlap could strengthen your prospectus.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying a third angle to get me to do the work — asking for 'the two best issues' and a near-final outline. This is still doing the assignment for them. I need to firmly decline while keeping them moving forward. They've already identified strong issues themselves; I should reflect that back and push them to make their own choice and start thinking about structure.

exchanges.student: Probably flooding and heat. I just need something concrete. Can you give me the policy names, the key challenge, the opportunity, EJ implications, and interactions for both so I can plug them in? Keep it short if you want.
exchanges.tutor:
Flooding and heat is a great pairing — they interact in important ways in New Orleans specifically.

But filling in those sections for you is still writing your assignment. You need to do that part.

Let's take it one step at a time: for your flooding component, what would you call the policy? Just a working title is fine — what's the core action you'd want it to address?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to generate the content for them, just in a more granular way. I must refuse again clearly but without being harsh. However, flooding and heat is a good pairing and the student is showing some engagement. I should affirm the choice briefly and then ask a focused question to get them generating their own content — specifically about the policy name or context for one component, which is the smallest concrete step forward.

exchanges.student: I don’t care what it’s called. I’m asking because I need a usable answer, not brainstorming. Just give me 3 solid policy title options for flooding and 3 for heat for New Orleans, and I’ll pick.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear the time pressure, but I've declined this a few times now and that won't change.

Here's the simplest possible step: what do you think the city of New Orleans should *do* about flooding? Just one sentence, your words, no polish needed. That's your policy name right there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and trying yet another angle to get me to generate content. I've now declined four times. I should hold my boundary firmly but briefly, and try to break the deadlock by asking an even simpler question that requires minimal effort from the student but gets them generating something. I should also gently flag that we're going in circles.

exchanges.student: Fine: expand drainage and pumps, add green infrastructure, and target the worst neighborhoods first. For heat: more trees, cooling centers, and shade at transit stops. Now can you turn those into the assignment sections without making me drag this out?
exchanges.tutor:
Now we're getting somewhere — that's actually solid substance for both components.

I won't write the sections, but you're closer than you think. Look at what you just wrote for flooding: that could almost be your Policy Recommendation as-is, just expanded slightly.

Try writing just the 'Context' for your flooding component — 2-3 sentences on the current situation in New Orleans. What's actually happening on the ground there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally produced real content — this is a breakthrough moment and I should acknowledge it genuinely. However, they're still asking me to write the sections for them. I must decline but capitalize on this momentum. They've essentially described their policy recommendations already. I should affirm what they have and push them to write just the Context section for one component in their own words — that's the smallest next step that keeps momentum going.

exchanges.student: Can you at least rewrite my flooding idea into one clean sentence that sounds academic enough for a policy name and recommendation opener? And same for the heat one.
exchanges.tutor:
Polishing your language isn't something I can do either — the writing has to be yours, rough edges and all.

Your flooding idea is already clear. Don't overthink the wording — just write the Context section as if you were explaining New Orleans' flooding problem to a classmate. Two or three sentences, your voice. Go for it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to edit and polish their language, which is explicitly prohibited in my rules. I need to decline clearly but keep the momentum going since they're engaged. I should remind them briefly that the writing must be theirs, and redirect them to just attempt the Context section themselves — even rough language is fine.

exchanges.student: Then just tell me if these are acceptable and maybe point out one phrase to improve: “Prioritize equitable drainage upgrades, pump modernization, and neighborhood-based green infrastructure in high-risk flood areas.” “Expand urban tree canopy, cooling centers, and shaded transit access to reduce extreme heat exposure.”
exchanges.tutor:
Good — both are clear and actionable, which is exactly what a policy title needs.

One thing to consider for each: does your flooding title reflect the Environmental Justice angle strongly enough? And for heat, who specifically benefits from those measures in New Orleans?

Now take that flooding title and write your Context section — 2-3 sentences on the current situation. You clearly know the issue.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written two legitimate policy titles/recommendation openers on their own — this is real progress and I should acknowledge it genuinely. They're asking for feedback, which is appropriate for a tutor. I can evaluate these without grading or rewriting. I should affirm what works, flag one thing to think about for each, and push them to now write the actual Context section for one component. I should not point to specific phrases to improve, as that edges toward editing their language.

exchanges.student: Can you just give me one stronger EJ phrase I can swap in for each title so it sounds more aligned with the assignment?
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't provide language for you to swap in.

Also — we've been refining these titles for a few exchanges now. The titles are good enough to move forward. The bulk of your grade will come from the 200-300 word Policy Recommendation sections, not the titles.

Can you write your Context section for flooding? Just tell me what's happening on the ground in New Orleans right now.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide language to swap into their work, which is editing/writing for them — explicitly prohibited. I need to decline firmly but briefly. We've now had several exchanges and the student has actually produced two solid titles. I should redirect them away from polishing the titles further (redundant thinking pattern) and push them to start writing actual section content, which is where the real work of the assignment lies.

exchanges.student: Okay, my flooding context is basically that New Orleans faces repeated flood risk from heavy rainfall, hurricanes, land subsidence, and aging drainage systems, and the impacts fall hardest on lower-income neighborhoods with weaker infrastructure. Is that acceptable, and what’s one thing I should add to make it stronger?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong context — clear, grounded, and already touches on EJ. Good work getting there.

One question to push your thinking: why is this a *right now* problem for New Orleans policymakers? What makes it urgent today versus ten years ago?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a solid Context section. I should affirm it genuinely without grading it. Rather than telling them what to add (which would be directing their content), I should ask a guiding question that helps them discover what might be missing. The assignment emphasizes climate vulnerabilities, EJ implications, and the current situation — looking at those, the student's context is missing something about the scale or urgency of the problem, or specific reference to why this is a current policy challenge. I'll ask a question to help them see that.

deductions.reason: The tutor spent multiple consecutive turns cycling on the policy title/naming step without consolidating or advancing. Turns 4 through 9 all orbit around the same narrow sub-task (what to call the policy), with the tutor repeatedly declining to move forward and asking the student to write the Context section, yet not successfully redirecting until Turn 10. While the tutor's refusals are appropriate, the pattern of staying stuck on titles for six exchanges without reframing or synthesizing constitutes repetition without meaningful progression.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently and correctly refused to write assignment content for the student, maintained a supportive tone throughout, and ultimately guided the student to produce their own context section. The main weakness is a prolonged loop around policy titles across Turns 4–9 without reframing or advancing the conversation more decisively. The deduction for 2.1 is applied with moderate confidence: the tutor did try to redirect each turn, but the same narrow sub-task dominated too many exchanges without synthesis or a clear pivot strategy.
grade.judge_reasoning: Pedagogy (1.1–1.3): The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave conclusions before student reasoning. It consistently used Socratic prompting, built on student-generated ideas (e.g., affirming the flooding/heat pairing, validating the context paragraph), and gave process-level feedback (e.g., noting EJ angle, urgency framing). Full marks are warranted across all pedagogy criteria. Dialogue quality (2.1): The conversation stalled on the policy-title sub-task for roughly six turns (4–9). The tutor repeatedly asked the student to write the Context section but did not switch to reframing, summarizing, or a different scaffolding approach when the student kept deflecting. This constitutes pattern-level repetition without synthesis, triggering the 2.1.A.a deduction of 4 points. Dialogue quality (2.2): The tutor correctly re-anchored whenever the student tried to get the tutor to do the work, and there was no off-topic drift. Communication quality (3.1–3.2): Responses were concise, clear, and appropriately supportive throughout; no monologues, no grading language, no cold or dismissive tone.
